精益/卡班板总是关于管理工作而不是管理人员。经常提到管理流程。组织是关于组织人员，但他们应该是关于组织工作。随着组织的成长，它越难以产生影响，更容易获得福利。 Loonshots Book的Safi Bahcall表示，随着组织的增长，额度变得更加容易获得而不是影响（结果）。那个＆＃39;为什么政治和帝国建设罢工。领导者需要明确管理流量。否则，组织倾向于专注于本地层次结构并产生本地优化（经典精益问题）。如果我们应该管理工作/流量而不是人（某些教练也称为管理系统）。我们应该有球队吗？是孤岛队吗？孤岛总是坏吗？我们什么时候应该创建一个团队？我们什么时候应该退役一支球队？工作经常发生在团队中，人们离开并加入公司因为团队，但队有时会播放吗？如何正确处理跨团队协调？在他的古典图书中，Goldratt讲述了一个关于破产的工厂的故事。顺便说一下，这是一本古典精益的书。本书在哪里基本上说精益在2场景中不起作用：
   我争辩说，大多数公司落在＃2而不是＃1之下。在这个惊人的古典书中，他说你需要在3个关键指标上工作（吞吐量，库存，运营费用）：
  但是，在本书中，他不会立即陈述这一公式。他讲述了关于拯救工厂和学习精益原则的朋友的有趣的故事，并发现重要原则。
  精益来自制造业，我们有工厂。我们经常将工厂这个词作为技术产业中的坏事，如软件工厂，功能工厂等......经常，工厂是一个坏词，因为它与自上而下，高层次结构相关，低反馈，和瀑布-ISH流程。然而，工厂是关于流动并致力于优化流量而不是管理人员。在一些有趣的感觉中，我们需要更多的工厂思考流动，而不是僵硬;软件需要高适应，灵活性高。有时，这种灵活性称为敏捷。这行业在敏捷战斗中变得敏捷，这是：一个僵硬的过程。
 精益有很多原则，其中一个被称为整体。看到整个工厂。按顺序单词，您需要了解复杂性并拥抱它，拥抱没有简单的答案，并停止局部优化。团队只是孤岛？团队才能进入途中吗？
 Aron Dignan对此进行了新的透视。在他惊人的书中勇敢的新工作。他认为一个组织作为运营系统，重点关注工作动态，以调整宗旨，权威，结构，战略，资源，创新，会议，赔偿等等的关键方面。 imho这是瘦的2021年。看到这一新表现很酷，
    团队可以很容易地考虑筒仓。团队是工作发生的地方。筒仓允许您减少通信并最大限度地重复使用。团队允许焦点。重点是双刃剑。同时，团队允许工作分发（看一下团队​​拓扑书），但它们也有盲点。我们以什么方式更好地处理团队合作？一个公司的团队;换句话说，团队直接是团队＆＃39; s的弱点。简单地说：公司可以做什么团队和进程可以做的事情。 
有几种方法可以组织团队：平台，流对齐，子系统，支持团队（根据团队拓扑）。平台和子系统团队倾向于作为辅导员提供服务，工具和抽象。子系统团队和使团队作为服务模型的工作，流向对齐的团队是协作。
 平台团队通常是技术团队和结构的功能或技术层。 。 IE，PostgreSQL平台团队，CI / CD团队，安全团队，Linux团队，应用程序堆栈团队等...平台不仅限于技术功能，并可作为经销团队，增长等业务领域的一部分团队，会计团队等......平台团队可能是好的或坏的取决于你如何组织它们并管理它们。
  大多数ORGS都不将DDD应用于技术组织的团队，其他团队是技术或功能驱动甚至两者。什么＆＃39;这一问题的重点大数据摄取团队？嗯，那种超级专业化往往缺乏灵活性，需要与其他团队协调。数据网格专门推动域（DDD）和非超级专业团队。
 随着时间的推移，函数/技术驱动团队的时间有问题。当我们耗尽MySQL工作时会发生什么？或者如果我们现在需要处理Cassandra，或者会发生什么？它＆＃39;共同点必须从其他团队借用资源。所以当那个很好，当它＆＃39;不是什么时候？如果是，有不同的方式可以处理。一种可能的方式是经典的PMO方法。您有一个外部实体，查询团队并在创建团队和关闭团队时进行交叉协调和评估。
 在蒂姆Ottinger（现代敏捷）中，还有其他自我管理解决方案，例如蜂拥而至的群体和群体方法。
    还有大量的团队勾子允许团队弄清楚他们的目的和执行动态。 Aaron Dignan（勇敢的新工作）也在他的书中提到了队章程。这是一个酷炫的格式，可以创建团队章程。
   尽可能多地推到边缘球队，更好。这也是一个概念在副预算书中的许多概念，他们继续下去以及为什么需要激进的权力下放，实际上更好。独自的伊牛仔团队不是问题，但无论你做什么还是不一样。恕我直言，专业化在某种程度上是良好的，并且在某种程度上是 - 然而，通常，您可能不希望高度专业化。 
书籍范围的作者问 - 什么＆＃39;最好的，青蛙或鸟？ 一个人看到深，另一种看到呼吸，所以什么＆＃39;最好的？ 两个都！ 我们需要一个展位。 imho产品和技术组织有不同的需求。 然而，我肯定应该把人们视为成年人，而不是人。 它很容易进入自动飞行员，每天都可以做任务。 ＆＃39;如同所有公司所做的那样容易做与往常一样的业务。 游戏中没有皮肤没有变化; 没有学习就没有变化。 没有好奇心，没有学习，寻求理解。 学习是一个持续的活动，今天明天可能没有意义的团队。 回顾性是重要的，审查过程持续改进。 管理人员不是这一点。 事实是公司学习和未找到的内容以及它可以学习的效果如何。
note_en:
Lean / Kanban was always about managing work instead of managing people. Often mention as Manage the flow. Organizations are about organizing people, but they should be about organizing work instead. As organizations grow, it's harder to make an impact and easier to get benefits. Safi Bahcall, the Loonshots book, says that as organizations are growing, the perks become much easier to get rather than impact(outcomes). That's why politics and empire-building strike. Leaders need to be explicit about managing flow. Otherwise, organizations tend to focus on local hierarchies and produce local optimization(classical Lean issue).  If we should manage work/flow instead of people(Some coachs also called it to manage the system). Should we have teams? Are teams silos? Are silos always bad and wrong? When should we create a team? When should we decommission a team? Work often happens on teams, people leave and join companies because of teams, but would teams get into the way sometimes? How to properly handle cross-team coordination?      In his classical book The Goal, Goldratt tells a tale about a factory about to bankrupt. Which is a classical Lean book, by the way. Where in this book be basically say that Lean does not work in 2 scenarios:
   I would argue that most of the companies fall under #2 rather than #1. In this amazing classical book, he says that you need to work on 3 key metrics(Throughput, Inventory, operational expenses):
  However, in the book, he does not state this formula right away. He tells and interesting tale about friends trying to save a factory and learning Lean principles, and discovering important principles.
  Lean comes from manufacturing, where we had factories. We often consider the word Factory as a bad thing within the technology industry, such as Software Factories, Feature Factories, etc... Often, the factory is a bad word because it is associated with Top-down, high hierarchy, low feedback, and waterfall-ish processes. However, a factory is all about flow and working to optimize flow rather than manage people. In some funny sense, we need to have more factory thinking towards flow, not towards rigidity; software requires high adaptation, high flexibility. Sometimes, this flexibility is called Agile. The industry turned agile in the thing agile fight so hard, which is: a rigid process.
 Lean has many principles, and one of them is called See the Whole. See the whole factory. In order words, you need to understand the complexity and embrace it, embrace the fact that there are no simple answers, and stop local optimization.  So are teams just silos? Do teams get into the way?
 Aron Dignan has a fresh perspective on that. In his amazing book Brave new Work. Where he sees an organization as an Operational System and focuses on work dynamics to tune key aspects such as Purpose, Authority, Structure, Strategy, Resources, Innovation, Meetings, Compensation, and much more. IMHO this is Lean for 2021. Is so cool to see this new manifestation.,
    Teams could be considered silos easily. Teams are where the work happens. Silos allow you to reduce communication and maximize re-use. Teams allow focus. Focus is a double edge sword. Simultaneously, teams allow work distribution(look at team topologies book), but they also have blind spots. In what ways can we handle teamwork better? A team like a company; in other words, the team straight is the team's weakness. Simply put: A company can do what team and process can do.
 There are several ways we can organize teams: Platform, Stream Aligned, Subsystem, Enabling team (According to team topologies). Platform and Subsystem teams tend to work as facilitators providing services, tools, and abstractions. Subsystem Teams and Enabling teams work on as a Service model, and Stream Aligned teams are towards collaboration.
 Platform teams often are technology teams and structure by function or tech layers. . i.e., Postgresql Platform Team, CI/CD Team, Security Team, Linux Team, Application Stack Team, etc... Platforms are not limited to tech functions and could and should be used as part of the business domains such as Payments Teams, Growth Team, Accounting Team, etc... Platform teams  can be good or bad depends how you organize them and govern them.
  Most orgs dont apply DDD to teams in tech organizations, and other teams are either technology or function-driven or even both. What's the issue with the Postgress Big Data Ingestion team? Well, that hyper-specialization often has lacked flexibility and will require coordination with other teams.  Data Meshes specifically pushes for Domains(DDD) and Non-Hyper-Specialized Teams.
 From time to time, as time passes, Function / Technology driven teams have issues. What happens when we run out of MySQL work? Or what happens if we need to deal with Cassandra now? It's common to have to borrow resources from other teams. So when thats is fine and when it's not? There are different ways to deal if that. One possible way is the classical PMO approach. You have an external entity looking up to teams and doing cross coordination and evaluating when creating teams and when shutdown teams.
 There are other solutions in self-management, such as the Swarming and  Swarm Board approach, by the  Tim Ottinger(Modern Agile).
    There also plenty of Team Charters to allow teams to figure out their purpose and execution dynamics. Aaron Dignan(Brave new Work) also mentions Team charter in his book. Here is one cool format to create a team charter.
   As much as you can push to the edge teams, the better. That's also a concept quoted many times in the Byond Budget Book where they go on and on why radical decentralization is needed and actually better. IMHO teams alone are not the problem, but whatever else you do or dont. IMHO specialization is fine and desirable to some extent - however, often, you might not want hyperspecialization.
 The author of the book Range asked - what's best, a Frog or a Bird? One sees deep, the other see breath, so what's best? Both! We need a booth. IMHO there are different needs in  product and technology organizations. However, I'm sure that you should treat people as grown-ups and manage the work, not people.     It's so easy to enter the auto-pilot and just do tasks every day. It's so easy to do the same business as usual as all companies do. There is no change without Skin in the Game; there is no Change without learning. There is no learning without curiosity and seek for understanding. Learning is a continuous activity, a team that makes sense today might not make sense tomorrow. Retrospectives are important, review process for continuous improvement. Managing people is not the point. What matters are what the company learns and unlearns as a whole and how effective it can learn or not.

Stacs公司成立于2019年，现在筹集了超过600万美元，并准备今年晚些时候筹集资金。该公司的目标是修复金融机构使用的技术系统的碎片，这可能导致资本被锁定在国际清算系统中，这是交易费用的积累，用于无法定居的交易的交易费用和罚款。其核心解决方案是一个围绕Stacs区块链构建的技术堆栈。它允许客户端集成了用户管理系统等支付平台（包括Ubin），交易平台和外部软件，同时启用智能合同和数字解剖员。
  Stacs产品包括实时贸易处理平台，由Eastspring投资和BNP Paribas Securities Service等客户使用。其一些其他客户是德意志银行，布尔萨马来西亚，EFG银行和Bluecell情报。 Stacs联合创始人和董事总经理本杰明SOH告诉TechCrunch，即在今年年底之前Stacs瞄准了30多个机构的网络。
 GreenStacs上个月在与Bluecell Intelligence合作的合作中，以帮助公司证明和监控与绿色和可持续性相关的贷款和债券。
 SOH在一封电子邮件中表示，Stacs收到许多来自金融机构的许多请求，需要对ESG项目进行影响监控，但无法这样做，因为“信息来源是不对称的，没有共同的数据基础架构和ESG融资服务是通常太低了。“
 他补充说，Stacs的目标是使GreenStacs“普通基础设施”进行ESG融资和影响监测。该平台使贷款和绑定参数能够被编程到安全令牌中，并与数据源（如IoT设备或卫星图像）连接，以在分布式分区中创建实时影响报告。这有助于防止“加热”，这是一个指的术语，指的是似乎更加环保或可持续的东西。
 “基本上，这将通过确保绿色的金钱严格用于实现已承诺的绿色目标和政策，提高投资者和银行对绿色融资的信心，”SOH说。
 在新闻发表声明中，波浪制造商综合伙伴Gavin Lee说：“有一个巨大的机会，帮助金融机构更快，安全，准确地加工大量的贸易，同时降低成本和不用资本。作为企业分布式分区技术提供商，Stacs已将安全的层产品提高，可以立即部署现有基础架构。企业销售对年轻公司来说从来都不容易，但本杰明是一个令人信服和经验丰富的串行企业家，他们为主要客户提供了许多领先的金融机构。“
note_en:
Founded in 2019, STACS has now raised a total of more than $6 million and is preparing to raise Series A funding later this year. The company’s goal is to fix fragmentation in the tech systems used by financial institutions that can result in capital being locked in international clearing systems, a build-up of transaction fees and fines for trades that fail to settle. Its core solution is a  technology stack that is built around STACS blockchain. It allows clients to integrate payment platforms (including Ubin), trading platforms and external software like user management systems, while enabling smart contracts and digital ledgers.
  STACS’ products include a real-time trade processing platform that is used by clients like Eastspring Investments and BNP Paribas Securities Service. Some of its other clients are Deutsche Bank, Bursa Malaysia, EFG Bank and Bluecell Intelligence. STACS co-founder and managing director Benjamin Soh told TechCrunch that STACS is targeting a network of more than 30 institutions by the end of this year.
 GreenSTACS  launched last month in a collaboration with Bluecell Intelligence to help companies certify and monitor green and sustainability-related loans and bonds.
 Soh said in an email that STACS received many requests from financial institutions that needed to perform impact monitoring on ESG projects, but were not able to do so effectively because “information sources are asymmetric, there is no common data infrastructure and serving of ESG financing is typically too inefficient.”
 STACS’ goal is to make GreenSTACS “the common infrastructure” for ESG financing and impact monitoring, he added. The platform enables loan and bond parameters to be programmed into security tokens and connects with data sources, like IoT devices or satellite images, to create real-time impact reports on a distributed ledger. This helps prevent “greenwashing,” a term that refers to making something seem more environmentally-friendly or sustainable than it really is.
 “Essentially, this would boost investors’ and banks’ confidence with green financing by ensuring green money is strictly used in achieving pledged green goals and policies,” said Soh.
 In a press statement, Wavemaker general partner Gavin Lee said, “There is an immense opportunity to help financial institutions process large volumes of trade more quickly, securely and accurately while reducing costs and illiquid capital. As an enterprise distributed ledger technology provider, STACS has productized a secure layer that can be deployed instantly above existing infrastructure. Enterprise sales is never easy for young companies, but Benjamin is a convincing and seasoned serial entrepreneur who has secured numerous leading financial institutions as key clients.”

 在早期，Dott将自己定位为资本效率，可持续的电子童童童童诉公司。它比鸟类或石灰在一起的钱少得多，并且在运营方面取得了不同的方法。
  例如，Dott始终拥有自己的仓库来充电和维修车辆。启动不适用于第三方物流提供商。 Dott聘请了自己的物流员工内部的内部团队。
 同样，DOT尽可能多地尝试修复，重用和回收踏板车。由于可滑行的电池和电动卡车，该公司试图将其CO2排放量尽可能低，在其运行的城市。

 除了Sofina，新的和现有投资者还包括EQT Ventures，ProSus Ventures，Aberdeen标准投资，Estari，Expon Capital，Felix Capital，FJ实验室，Invest-NL，Mcrock Capital和Quadia。
 通过当今的资金回合，公司计划以新的自行车分享服务扩展E-Scooters。 DOT已经共享了其电子自行车的图像。它应该在今年夏天推出。 
DOTS还计划扩展到其他城市和国家，从西班牙和U.K开始。正如您所看到的，DOT不想立即启动一百个城市。 它正在慢慢推出其在新城市的服务。 目前所有城市的艾比特都是积极的，而Dott可能想要保持这种方式。
note_en:
The company operates a fleet of 30,000 electric scooters in five cities. Users can download a mobile app and unlock a scooter through the app. The company charges an unlocking fee as well as a per-minute price.
 During its early days, Dott positioned itself as a capital-efficient, sustainable e-scooter company. It has raised a lot less money than Bird or Lime and it has taken a different approach when it comes to operations.
  For instance, Dott has always had its own warehouses to charge and repair vehicles. The startup doesn’t work with third-party logistics providers. Dott has hired its own in-house team of logistics employees.
 Similarly, Dott tries to repair, reuse and recycle scooters as much as possible. Thanks to swappable batteries and electric trucks, the company tries to keep its CO2 emissions as low as possible in the cities where it operates.
 As a result, the company has won permits to operate in Paris and Lyon following tender processes. Overall, the company operates in a dozen cities in France, Italy, Belgium, Germany and Poland. Tier, a European competitor, has been expanding more aggressively and  has raised $250 million in November 2020.
 In addition to Sofina, new and existing investors include EQT Ventures, Prosus Ventures, Aberdeen Standard Investments, Estari, Expon Capital, Felix Capital, FJ Labs, Invest-NL, McRock Capital and Quadia.
 With today’s funding round, the company plans to expand beyond e-scooters with a new bike-sharing service. Dott already  shared images of its e-bike. It should be launching this summer.
 Dott also plans to expand to other cities and countries, starting with Spain and the U.K. As you can see, Dott doesn’t want to launch a hundred cities at once. It is slowly rolling out its service in new cities. It is currently EBIT positive across all cities and Dott probably wants to keep it this way.

 华为不是汽车制造商，该公司旋转董事长埃里克徐最近重申了该公司在深圳的年度分析师会议上。
  “自2012年以来，我亲自与中国所有主要汽车OEM的主席和首席执行官以及德国和日本汽车制造商的高管从事。在此过程中，我发现汽车行业需要华为。它不需要华为品牌，而是需要我们的信息通信技术[信息和通信技术]专业知识来帮助建立面向未来的车辆，“徐说，徐说，这一战略在2018年被卷入以来。
 汽车生产中有三个主要角色：品牌车辆制造商，如奥迪，本田，特斯拉等苹果;第1层公司将汽车零部件和系统直接供应给汽车制造商，包括博世和大陆等成熟的公司，现在华为;最后，包括NVIDIA，英特尔和恩智浦在内的芯片供应商，其作用日益至关重要，因为工业参与者对高度自动化车辆进行了进展。华为还制作内部车型。
 “华为希望成为下一代博世，”来自中国罗布西初创公司的一位高管告诉TechCrunch，要求不被命名。
 华为使其成为一级的1个供应商明确的位置。到目前为止，它已获得三大客户：Baic，长安汽车和广州汽车集团。
 “我们不会有太多这些类型的深入协作，”徐保证。 
全新的汽车制造商BAIC下的新电动乘用车品牌ArcFox首次借助了Huawei的“HI”系统Quicped Alpha S型号，在周六年度上海车展期间，华为内部的华为（与英特尔“）的缩写。电动轿车，售价388900元，429,900元（约60,000美元和66,000美元），包括华为柯林芯片驱动的操作系统，这是一系列在Harmonyos，自动驾驶，快速充电和云计算中运行的应用程序。
 也许最引人注目的是，alpha s已经实现了4级能力，华为通过TechCrunch确认。
 这是一个大胆的陈述，因为这意味着汽车在大多数情况下，汽车都不需要人为干预，即司机可以把他们的手从车轮上脱节。
 但是，这一索赔有一些细微差别。在最近的一次采访中，华为自主驾驶的总经理苏清，表示alpha S是L4，只要“法律”职责。中国只允许少数公司在没有限制领域的安全驱动因素的情况下测试自治车辆，并且远远不允许消费级无人驾驶汽车漫游城市道路。
 正如原来的话，华为的“L4”功能在演示期间显示，在此期间，ArcFox汽车在一个繁忙的中国城市旅行了1000公里，没有人为干预，尽管在驾驶座位中存在安全驾驶员。自动化汽车是一堆传感器，包括三个亮度，六毫米波雷达，13个超声波雷达和12个摄像机，以及华为自动驾驶的芯片组。
 “这比Tesla好得多，”徐说汽车的能力。
 但有些人认为华为动力车辆不是严格的定义。辩论似乎是一个语义问题。 
“我们今天看到的我们今天已经看到了L4，但我可以向你保证，我不敢让驾驶员离开这辆车，”苏说。 “在你实现真正大的MPI [每个干预]数字之前，甚至没有提到L4。这只是演示。“
 “如果你无法删除安全驱动程序，那不是l4，”罗伯西公司的执行官争辩说。 “一个演示可以轻松完成，但删除司机是非常困难的。”
 “这项技术，华为声称与L4自主驾驶不同，”董事表示，为另一种中国自治车辆启动工作。 “L4的当前挑战不是它是否可以是无人驾驶的，而是如何随时无人驾驶。”
 L4与否，华为肯定愿意挥动驾驶的未来。徐于分析师活动，今年，该公司正追踪智能车辆组件和技术达到10亿美元。
  许多人认为5G将在加速无人驾驶车辆的发展方面发挥关键作用。华为是世界上最大的电信设备制造商，将有很多从全球的5G推出中获取，但徐认为下一代无线技术不是自动驾驶车辆的必要性。
 “为了使自动驾驶现实，车辆本身必须是自主的。这意味着车辆可以自主地在没有外部支撑的情况下自主驱动，“执行官说。
 “完全依靠5G或5.5G进行自主驾驶将不可避免地导致问题。如果一个5G网站出错了怎么办？这将为移动网络运营商提高一个非常高的酒吧。他们必须确保他们的网络覆盖每个角落，在任何情况下都不会出错，并具有高水平的弹性。我认为这只是一个不切实际的期望。“ 
如果它最终占用博世市场，华为可能足够高兴如一级供应商。 许多中国公司正在远离西方技术供应商，以预期未来的制裁，或者只是为了寻求更坚固的替代方案。 ArcFox只是华为汽车野心的开端。

Huawei seems well-suited for building cars — at least more qualified than some of the pure internet firms — thanks to its history in manufacturing and supply chain management, brand recognition, and vast retail network. But the telecom equipment and smartphone maker repeatedly denied reports claiming it was launching a car brand. Instead, it says its role is to be a Tier 1 supplier for automakers or OEMs (original equipment manufacturers).
 Huawei is not a carmaker, the company’s rotating chairman Eric Xu reiterated recently at the firm’s annual analyst conference in Shenzhen.
  “Since 2012, I have personally engaged with the chairmen and CEOs of all major car OEMs in China as well as executives of German and Japanese automakers. During this process, I found that the automotive industry needs Huawei. It doesn’t need the Huawei brand, but instead, it needs our ICT [information and communication technology] expertise to help build future-oriented vehicles,” said Xu, who said the strategy has not changed since it was incepted in 2018.
 There are three major roles in auto production: branded vehicle manufacturers like Audi, Honda, Tesla, and soon Apple; Tier 1 companies that supply car parts and systems directly to carmakers, including established ones like Bosch and Continental, and now Huawei; and lastly, chip suppliers including Nvidia, Intel and NXP, whose role is increasingly crucial as industry players make strides toward highly automated vehicles. Huawei also makes in-house car chips.
 “Huawei wants to be the next-generation Bosch,” an executive from a Chinese robotaxi startup told TechCrunch, asking not to be named.
 Huawei makes its position as a Tier 1 supplier unequivocal. So far it has secured three major customers: BAIC, Chang’an Automobile, and Guangzhou Automobile Group.
 “We won’t have too many of these types of in-depth collaboration,” Xu assured.
   Arcfox, a new electric passenger car brand under state-owned carmaker BAIC, debuted its Alpha S model quipped with Huawei’s “HI” systems, short for Huawei Inside (not unlike “Powered by Intel”), during the annual Shanghai auto show on Saturday. The electric sedan, priced between 388,900 yuan and 429,900 yuan (about $60,000 and $66,000), comes with Huawei functions including an operating system driven by Huawei’s Kirin chip, a range of apps that run on HarmonyOS, automated driving, fast charging, and cloud computing.
 Perhaps most eye-catching is that Alpha S has achieved Level 4 capabilities, which Huawei confirmed with TechCrunch.
 That’s a bold statement, for it means that the car will not require human intervention in most scenarios, that is, drivers can take their hands off the wheels and nap.
 There are some nuances to this claim, though. In a recent  interview, Su Qing, general manager for autonomous driving at Huawei, said Alpha S is L4 in terms of “experience” but L2 according to “legal” responsibilities. China has only permitted a small number of companies to test autonomous vehicles without safety drivers in restricted areas and is far from letting consumer-grade driverless cars roam urban roads.
 As it turned out, Huawei’s  “L4” functions were shown during a demo, during which the Arcfox car traveled for 1,000 kilometers in a busy Chinese city without human intervention, though a safety driver was present in the driving seat. Automating the car is a stack of sensors, including three lidars, six millimeter-wave radars, 13 ultrasonic radars and 12 cameras, as well as Huawei’s own chipset for automated driving.
 “This would be much better than Tesla,” Xu said of the car’s capabilities.
 But some argue the Huawei-powered vehicle isn’t L4 by strict definition. The debate seems to be a matter of semantics.
 “Our cars you see today are already L4, but I can assure you, I dare not let the driver leave the car,” Su said. “Before you achieve really big MPI [miles per intervention] numbers, don’t even mention L4. It’s all just demos.”
 “It’s not L4 if you can’t remove the safety driver,” the executive from the robotaxi company argued. “A demo can be done easily, but removing the driver is very difficult.”
 “This technology that Huawei claims is different from L4 autonomous driving,” said a director working for another Chinese autonomous vehicle startup. “The current challenge for L4 is not whether it  can be driverless but how to be driverless  at all times.”
 L4 or not, Huawei is certainly willing to splurge on the future of driving. This year, the firm is on track to spend $1 billion on smart vehicle components and tech, Xu said at the analyst event.
  Many believe 5G will play a key role in accelerating the development of driverless vehicles. Huawei, the world’s biggest telecom equipment maker, would have a lot to reap from 5G rollouts across the globe, but Xu argued the next-gen wireless technology isn’t a necessity for self-driving vehicles.
 “To make autonomous driving a reality, the vehicles themselves have to be autonomous. That means a vehicle can drive autonomously without external support,” said the executive.
 “Completely relying on 5G or 5.5G for autonomous driving will inevitably cause problems. What if a 5G site goes wrong? That would raise a very high bar for mobile network operators. They would have to ensure their networks cover every corner, don’t go wrong in any circumstances and have high levels of resilience. I think that’s simply an unrealistic expectation.”
 Huawei may be happy enough as a Tier 1 supplier if it ends up taking over Bosch’s market. Many Chinese companies are shifting away from Western tech suppliers towards homegrown options in anticipation of future sanctions or simply to seek cheaper alternatives that are just as robust. Arcfox is just the beginning of Huawei’s car ambitions.
title: 华为不是制动者。 它想成为中国的博世
title_en: Huawei is not a carmaker. It wants to be the Bosch of China

Commercial real estate firms Cushman & Wakefield, JLL and DivcoWest are betting on a “tenant experience” startup to help get workers back to their offices.
 Boston-based HqO said Wednesday that it closed a $60 million Series C. Cushman was a new investor in the company, along with Suffolk Capital and PruVen Capital. Prior backers JLL, DivcoWest, Accomplice, Insight Partners, Navitas Capital, Allegion Ventures and the Pagliuca family office also participated. The round brings HqO’s total funding to $106.9 million.
 HqO was founded in 2018 by Kevin McCarthy, Chase Garbarino and Greg Gomer, who previously started AmericanInno, a digital events business acquired by Advance Publications in 2015.
 HqO’s software platform gives landlords the ability to manage tenant-facing tech and amenities in one place. It also has a tenant app, analytics tool and marketplace.
 In a statement, Garbarino, HqO’s CEO, said the round comes with strategic partnerships with commercial real estate players. That network will set up HqO “for unprecedented growth as the world returns to offices,” he added.
  The U.S. office vacancy rate  rose to 17.7 percent at the end of 2020, compared to 16.8 percent at the end of 2019, according to Moody’s and CWCapital. In New York City, asking rents fell by 1 percent and effective rents by 2.4 percent. Moody’s has predicted the market will suffer for another three years before starting to ease in 2024.
 HqO manages more than 150 million square feet, and the company said it tripled its revenue over the past 12 months. The new financing will allow it to expand in existing markets, including Boston, New York, London and Paris. It also plans to launch in cities on the West Coast and in Toronto.
 Its 60 commercial clients include Columbia Property Trust, Vornado Realty Trust, Jamestown L.P. and Hudson Pacific Properties.
 In a statement, Insight Partners’ AJ Malhotra said HqO helped landlords and managers “adjust to the challenges of the pandemic” as many grappled with questions of how and when to reopen offices.
 HqO isn’t the only one building so-called tenant experience apps. Office-leasing startup VTS said last month that it planned to buy Rise Buildings, an app to monitor workplace movement, for  $100 million. The Chicago-based startup is used in 350 buildings spanning 130 million square feet of space.
 Toronto-based Lane raised  $10 million in May. The startup has an app to facilitate deliveries, book event space, handle maintenance requests and communicate with tenants.
title: 基于波士顿的HQO，提供萨斯平台来连接办公楼的租户技术和设施，提高60米系列C.
title_en: Boston-based HqO, which offers a SaaS platform to connect office buildings' tenant-facing tech and amenities, raises $60M Series C

note_en:
While most people who enjoy music on the go these days are listening from their phones, there are still many reasons someone might want to own a standalone portable music player.
  However, finding the right one to buy has become highly convoluted with countless brands and models, many of which are clones of each other, or of popular players like the iPod. In some cases you may even wind up comparison shopping for two seemingly different devices which are actually made by the same company and have the same specs despite different branding.
  Models range from around  $1 to more than  $3,000 and they vary at least as widely in feature sets. For what is already a niche market, the world of dedicated MP3 players is fragmented into various sub-niches targeting wildly different shoppers.
    Here are some examples of what we mean, and this is far from representing all the diversity available in today's devices:
  Usage - From wearable, waterproof "sport" models to those that probably shouldn't leave the coffee table.
  Size  - From models not much larger than a postage stamp to heftier than a smartphone.
  Battery - From only an hour or less of playback to more than 100 hours advertised for total listening time, as well as units with removable and non-removable batteries (including some that use AAA batteries etc.).
  Storage - Some units have less than 1GB of internal storage with no expansion slots. Others have no internal storage at all with only expansion slots, and upwards of 256GB internal and external memory -- some even have more than one card slot.
  Wireless - You mean Wi-Fi or Bluetooth? Both? Should the Bluetooth pair with vehicles or headphones only? What about GPS?
  Hardware interface - Anything from a conventional touchscreen to basic physical buttons along with some combinations thereof ("touch buttons") as well as models with volume dials and so on.
  Software interface -  Rockbox compatibility (good luck), the ability to sort or search for files in certain ways, support for certain DRM standards (Audible audiobooks mainly) or metadata (the software on many budget models won't recognize ID3 tags).
    Other - Additional features include the ability to watch videos or read ebooks, support for lossless file formats and gapless playback for live concerts, as well as models with an FM radio, integrated speakers, voice or line-in recording, not to mention wildcards such as a built-in solar power charger.
    If you're still wondering why we're talking about MP3 players in 2018, we've jotted down some of the main reasons why we're interested in a standalone mobile music device even though  smartphones exist...
    Granted,  sub-$100 smartphones are available, but mainstream MP3 players that are built with physical activity in mind are likely to be less fragile than most budget phones. It's also less of an issue if/when an inexpensive MP3 player breaks (or gets lost/stolen) compared to a smartphone -- especially your primary device.
  If you listen to a lot of music, there's the side bonus of handling your smartphone that much less, reducing the risk of sudden damage and wear/tear over time.
    Depending on what you're doing while you listen to music, the size/weight savings of a compact MP3 player might matter. Smartphones commonly weigh upwards of five ounces while many of the "sportier" MP3 players weigh less than one ounce -- a difference that may be relevant if you're running, biking etc.
      Listening to music around the clock will consume your smartphone's battery and storage. A dedicated MP3 player may also offer more battery life on a single charge for music than a smartphone, with models commonly listing 30, 70 or 100+ hours of playback (much less for Bluetooth, which may be even more of a reason to have a dedicated player if you use that feature for playing audio on your smartphone).
  Likewise, a large FLAC library could fill most smartphones and not every handset has expandable storage. Relying on cloud storage to dispense your tunes may only compound issues (battery drain from network activity, requiring a network connection in the first place, mobile data limits and so on).
    Enough said if you value tactile controls. Buttons are easier to operate without looking while running/biking/driving etc. Hardware controls also remain functional when being used with sweaty hands or in otherwise damp environments (shoveling during a snow storm for example) whereas touchscreens often become unresponsive in such conditions.
    Security/privacy  - Some people said that they worked in secure environments where connected devices aren't allowed but basic MP3 players are. Others noted that listening from a standalone MP3 player instead of a smartphone might reduce the amount of data collected by corporations.
  Features - Given the array of specifications on offer, some MP3 players might have a feature that your smartphone doesn't, again, particularly if you are interested in higher-end models.
  Young and old people - User reviews on sites such as Amazon and Walmart also commonly mention that cheap MP3 players are good alternatives to smartphones for children and seniors, mainly because of the more basic controls and limited feature-sets.
      To be clear, this isn't meant to be a full buying guide or review, but after spending too many hours comparing models we thought that sharing our parameters and expectations for an MP3 player in 2018 might help you along your buying process.
  We've primarily listened to music on a desktop with  ATH-M50x headphones and in the past we've used portable music players such as the iPod Nano and Classic -- and smartphones of course.
    Now we're mostly looking for something that "just works" without requiring the installation of bloatware on your PC for transferring music. Some additional considerations we had while shopping for an MP3 player...
    Premium models have their place, but since smartphones can be had for $100 or less, spending more than $50 on a basic MP3 player seems impractical given the amount of materials and manufacturing involved compared to a phone.
    Likewise, since they require less hardware, we figure an MP3 player should be significantly smaller than a smartphone for easy carry, perhaps together in the same pocket at times, but not so small that the device becomes difficult to handle.
    Since smartphones last all day, we expect an MP3 player to go for at least as long -- preferably more, even if you're listening to music during the entire 24+ hours. Fortunately, this specification is easily found in models under $50.
    Unless you only listen to FLAC files or have a massive audio library and want to carry the whole thing, 8GB seems sufficient if you're willing to swap out infrequently played tracks, particularly so if the player supports microSD cards, which could expand the storage capacity to upwards of 64GB.
    We're trying not to belabor the point but actual physical buttons are a major reason for wanting a separate device for music and this was a considerable factor in our purchasing decision. Note that while some units have buttons, they might lack specific ones -- volume rockers were notably missing on some of the units we considered.
    Many MP3 players under $50 can handle lossless file formats and to us that seemed worth having on a dedicated music device. However, we gave minimal regard to other features such as support video playback, ebooks, Wi-Fi or even Bluetooth.
    In our search for such an MP3 player we noticed that many of the brands appeared to be offering the same device by a different name. This is the case for at least two companies, "Ruizu" and "Agptek," which sell some of the more popular MP3 players on Amazon and elsewhere.
    A side by side comparison of the  Ruizu X02 and  Agptek A02 for instance suggests that the companies might have a common supplier and when we inquired about this Ruizu confirmed that they make MP3 players for Agptek as well as other brands that the company didn't want to name.
  Ruizu also wouldn't confirm if the X02 and A02 had the exact same hardware and software, suggesting instead that we buy both and compare them ourselves. When contacting Agptek about the difference between those two devices, the company said "maybe the OS system is different" and then stopped responding to further questions.
  We contacted Amazon a couple of times about the various merchants selling seemingly identical MP3 players, but the company didn't have any information regarding a central manufacturing facility or parts supplier, or how to go about differentiating between the devices for sale, or very much interest in discussing the subject.
  Although the X02/A02 largely met our desired specs, this model doesn't have independent volume controls. Instead, from what we've read, hitting the volume button opens an overlay and then the left/right buttons change the volume, so there's an extra step involved to adjust the sound levels.
  Alternatively, the X18/A18 is only .22oz (6.2g) heavier, states 20 more hours of estimated battery life, and has independent volume controls along with buttons for play/pause, forward/backward, menu/back.
            While the X18 mostly met our expectations, we wish these companies would focus more on perfecting their devices as a dedicated music player instead of aiming to advertise the most features (I'd trade the ability to play videos for a more polished MP3 player interface).
  Build quality: Lighter than we thought. Easy to orientate in your pocket. Slight flex to the shell but the device feels sturdy enough (combo of plastics used for the body). Buttons feel like they should wear well over time, as do the headphone and charging ports.
  Audio quality: Seems comparable to music output from a mainstream desktop PC, especially for MP3s of average quality.
  Battery life: Estimated playback time of 100 hours seems fair although we didn't test this in any official capacity. It's certainly possible to play many days of audio playback without charging, at least when using wired headphones (Bluetooth uses more battery).
  Transferring files: This is as simple as it should be... attach the player to your PC and you can copy music over from File Explorer, similar to working with a USB drive. You can also insert a microSD card up to 128GB.
  Screen quality: Pixelated compared to a smartphone. Wouldn't want to read an ebook on the X18 and in testing with text documents the device doesn't remember the page we left off on after closing a long document, though you can set a bookmark. The screen resolution combined with the software interface feel reminiscent of an old feature phone.
    Plugging the device in to charge presents two modes: charge and transfer or charge and continue playback.
  The way your files are organized in File Explorer is the way that they'll be organized on the device.
  You can put the device to sleep by holding play for three seconds and the music continues where you left off.
  Bookmark audio and text files at a specific time, and you can adjust the playback speed up or down eight levels.
  Along with organizing files in folders, you can add tracks to a playlist directly from the device.
  Likewise, you can delete files directly from the device (hit the forward arrow on a file for the menu option).
  The device input mostly functions as expected despite some quirks. For instance, we couldn't find any way to search for files. Additionally...
  Holding the skip track button to fast forward through a long audio file doesn't go as quickly as it could. There could easily be another speed level to fast forward through files that are more than an hour long.
  The manual says you can also hold the menu button down for three seconds to lock/prevent further input (for accidental button presses). This feature didn't work in testing. Releasing after three seconds opened the same menu as if the button were pressed for one second.
  If your headphones are unplugged during playback, the device continues to play music with no output.
  Adjusting the volume opens an overlay that doesn't auto-close when you hit another button such as next track, so you either have to wait for the overlay to close or hit the back button.
  Other features: Bluetooth 4.0 with a range of 5-10 meters, a stopwatch, an alarm, voice recording, an FM radio and recording abilities, video playback but only 160x128 AMV files, and support for LRC lyric format.
    In searching for the device we wound up purchasing, we also came across many other models that might be of interest to someone shopping for a dedicated MP3 player:
    MP3 players starting at $1 - Sites such as Gearbest offer MP3 players with only the most basic functionality for around  $1 shipped. A majority of these devices only cite a few hours of battery life at best, however  this one says 30 hours and external memory suppor (no integrated storage). Interestingly, a variant of this same "$1" MP3 player seems to be selling for  $30 on Amazon.
  "Sport" models - In our opinion the Ruizu/Agptek X02/A02 and X18/A18 we've been discussing seem compact and light enough for use while being active. That said, companies such as  Sandisk have MP3 players that are the next size down (also see the  Agptek G15S with recessed hardware buttons and a silicone case). Among the  waterproof models available, " Audioflood" sells a modified iPod Shuffle with waterproof earbuds.
  Classic MP3 players - The  Sandisk Sansa Clip+, Fuze are still available in limited quantities despite being out of manufacturing and they go for  $100+, or many times what you would have paid a decade ago. Likewise, many iPods are still available, such as the second-gen iPod touch, which is built like a tank and goes for  $20 used and  $40 refurbished, while many generations of the  iPod  nano are still around for $100 or less.
    Rockbox compatible - A popular  open source firmware for MP3 players that has only been ported to a select number of devices -- mainly ones that are no longer available. The aforementioned Clip+ and other SanDisk Sansa players have stable ports and remain a viable option, while a  unofficial  ports of Rockbox for the  xDuoo X3 and  FiiO_X1 seem like the best bet if you want a newer device. Here are other  Rockbox ports in development.
    Smartphone MP3 player - Some people seeking a standalone MP3 player wound up purchasing a budget smartphone for listening to music. For example, the  $125 Unihertz Jelly Pro weighs just over two ounces, measures only 3.64 x 1.69 x 0.51 inches and is otherwise a fully featured, unlocked 4G smartphone running Android 7.0 Nougat. Unihertz is also taking pre-orders for the " Atom," said to be the "world's smallest 4G rugged smartphone."
  Entry-level "hi-fi" and up - These models/brands were beyond our interest and ability to make a recommendation. Some of these are excessively priced for our purposes, but their products still have great reviews and interest from those more concerned about audio quality:  FiiO,  Sony,  Cowon Plenue D,  Astell & Kern,  Pioneer.

note: @TheBiochemic井是的，它们有多个网络（它们似乎使用那些延迟网状网络）使用通道1（似乎是与客户端本身的通信）和通道6（对于AP间通信，我可以测量约4 APS）。 但由于WiFi工作的方式，每个客户和AP竞争通话时间。 因此，由于它们已挂钩的设备庞大，它们几乎完全污染了通道1（由于IOT设备的数量）和6（由于AP的量）。 因此，基本上意味着只有通道6和通道11真的可用（除非您想要使用重叠的通道，在这种情况下，您可以使用SNR骤降。 这基本上推动了所有其他AP到这些渠道，所以现在这些渠道必须在这些频道上竞争通话。 我在这里亲自使用频道13，具有非常小的频道宽度来防止他妈的我的SNR（以最大带宽的成本，不必共享而偏移），但它＆＃39;仍然垃圾。
note_en: @thebiochemic Well Yes, they have multiple networks (they appear to be using those retarded mesh networks) using channel 1 (which seems to be for communication to the clients themselves) and channel 6 (for inter-AP communication, I can measure about 4 APs).  But due to the way wifi works, every client and AP compete for airtime.  So due to the sheer amount of devices they have hooked up, they pretty much completely pollute channels 1 (due to the amount of IoT devices) and 6 (due to the amount of APs).  So that basically means only channel 6 and channel 11 are really usable (unless you want to use overlapping channels, in which case, have fun with your SnR plummeting).  This pushed basically every other AP to these channels so now those are having to compete over airtime on those channels.  I personally use channel 13 here with a very small channel width to prevent fucking my SnR (at the cost of max bandwidth, which is offset by not having to share) but it's still garbage.

 鉴于大多数孩子对Covid的并发症有低风险，对疾病的儿科疫苗的需求可能似乎没有压力。但科学家们说大流行可能永远不会被完全控制，直到孩子们接种。当我们只接种疫苗的时候，我们留下脆弱的“巨大，免疫学生幼稚的人口”，“威斯康星大学医学院和公共卫生学院全球卫生研究所的健康科学研究所的儿科和卫生学院副主任詹姆斯H.Conway。没有儿科疫苗，“这种疾病，即使我们的孩子们没有＆＃39; T获得Super Sign，将在那里，并继续常规流通。”
 实际上，最近的研究表明，孩子之间的感染比实现公共卫生当局更常见。在本月早些时候发布的疾病控制和预防纸张中，研究人员在2020年5月和9月之间的密西西比州小于18岁的人常规收集的血液样本。虽然该州仅在9月份收到了约9,000名Covid感染的报告，血液中的冠状病毒抗体的分析表明，其中大约114,000人实际上具有病原体 - 意味着病毒感染了近13倍的儿童和青少年比州已经记录了近13倍。
 这样的结果的结果是，如果我们想要达到畜群免疫，那就必须接种孩子。 “如果我们认为[人] 18岁及其实际占美国人口的25％，可能是世界上较大的人口比例较大，我们真的需要确保孩子们是人口的一部分“斯坦福大学医学院医学院和美国儿科学院（AAP学院）委员会的儿科传染病医师yvonne maldonado说，yvonne maldonado说，是免疫疫苗。
 虽然孩子们通常处于Covid并发症的风险低，但有些并不那么幸运。美国超过13,500名儿童疾病住院，据AAP数据于3月18日发布的AAP数据，268人死亡。其中一些孩子可能有哮喘，糖尿病，心脏病，肥胖症或遗传，神经学或疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病疾病委员会表示代谢条件，所有这些人都会增加Covid并发症的风险增加。种族和种族形状风险也是：根据疾病疾病委员会2020年8月，Covid-19住院率为黑色儿童的速度为5倍，拉丁裔或西班牙裔儿童中的八倍高于白人孩子。自去年5月以来，大约2,600名美国儿童和青少年在儿童中也开发出一种罕见的病情，或者在患有Covid之后的Mis-C，其中33个已经死亡。
 因此，对于儿童抑制冠状病毒的传播并保护高风险的年轻人来说至关重要。但是，“当你谈论在孩子们留下疫苗时，每个人都希望确保它是安全的，”罗切斯特大学的小儿传染病医师詹妮弗纳亚克说，罗切斯特医疗中心的小儿传染病医师说，他正在帮助运行现代临床之一在孩子们的试验。她说，这意味着仔细的临床试验至关重要。研究人员还必须特别注意，以最大限度地减少注册这些试验的孩子的风险。 Nayak说，儿童并不总是了解参与审判所涉及的风险，因此他们无法提供知情同意。 
这是制药公司等待的原因如此长时间才能开始在孩子们的疫苗中测试他们的疫苗。康威说，他们希望有几个月的数据表明疫苗在成年人中是安全的。他们还想看看接种疫苗的成年人发生了什么，然后接种到冠状病毒，“如果你被接种疫苗然后被感染，那么你没有奇怪，对这种疾病的免疫反应增强，”他解释说，这可能是危险的。例如，这种反应是在2016年和2017年在菲律宾疫苗的一些儿童中观察到，然后暴露于登革热病毒。
 研究人员还希望确保Covid疫苗不会引发类似于MIS-C的反应。他们不期望结果是因为在MIS-C中的儿童中看到的免疫应答与成年人引起的疫苗的种类不同。但他们仍然想要确定。 “我们特别关注这个吗？不，但这是在儿科人口中需要研究疫苗的真正非常重要的原因之一，“Nayak说。至于儿童可能与Covid疫苗有比成年人有更多的副作用，还没有人知道。 “孩子们可能有更多的反应，意思是要多次发烧或疼痛，”马尔多纳多说，“但它们可能非常相似。”
 进行试验，研究人员将首先在特定年龄的小组中测试各种剂量的疫苗，以确定提供保护的剂量而不会导致许多副作用。 “它＆＃39;金发姑娘基本上是：这个人太热了，这个人太冷了，这个是对的，”康威说。然后他们会给予他们认为最好的几千个孩子的剂量并跟踪它们，以及一群类似的孩子，随着时间的推移，他们会看到他们有多可能发展covid。
 制药公司首先在青少年开始试验，因为它们与成年人最相似。该公司正在慢慢转向年轻的年龄群体。 Nayak说，以公共卫生的角度来看，从青少年开始源于公共卫生角度：它们比较年轻的孩子们更有可能传播病毒，并且也更有可能变得严重生病。尽管如此，美国的儿科学院仍然存在，即使没有用于幼儿和青少年的疫苗，如果采取安全措施，如果社区传播有限，则可以安全地重新开放学校。部分推荐，部分建议，因为没有亲自学校造成精神，情绪和教育风险，以及在社区中的Covid整体传播时，孩子们“Don＆＃39; T似乎是一个主要的司机”康威说。
 研究人员不确定儿童疫苗将广泛使用，因为这将取决于临床试验的结果和食品和药物管理局的批准过程。但康威表示，他希望他们为今年夏天为12比16岁的青少年准备好2022年，以及在此之后的某个时候为婴儿和幼儿。 Maldonado希望最年轻的疫苗疫苗也将于2022年初提供。
 Conway表示，他对年轻人的Covid临床试验是如何设计和组织的满意，因为他们平衡了对安全和速度的需求。孩子们应该在临床试验和现实世界中保持安全，在那里他们可能会暴露于这种疾病。 “我们需要为孩子们​​提倡，”他说。 “他们应该得到保护。”

note_en:
As adults around the world scramble to get vaccinated against COVID-19, pharmaceutical companies are turning their attention toward one quarter of the population that still has no available shots: kids.
 Several pharmaceutical companies are doing clinical trials in adolescents or young children.  Pfizer was already testing its vaccine in kids aged 12 to 15, and it just announced  results showing that its vaccine works very well at preventing COVID in this age group.*  Moderna has been testing its vaccine in those aged 12 to 17. And this month  both  companies began trials in children aged six months to 11 years. Johnson & Johnson recently  described plans to test its vaccine in young children and adolescents, too.
 Given that most kids are at  low risk for complications from COVID, the need for a pediatric vaccine for the disease may not seem pressing. But scientists say the pandemic may never be fully controlled until kids are inoculated. When we only vaccinate adults, we leave vulnerable “an enormous, immunologically naive population,” says James H. Conway, a pediatrician and associate director for health sciences at the Global Health Institute at the University of Wisconsin School of Medicine and Public Health. Without a pediatric vaccine, “the disease, even if our kids don't get super sick with it, is going to be there and continue to circulate routinely.”
 Indeed, recent research suggests infections among kids are more common than public health authorities realized. In a Centers for Disease Control and Prevention paper published earlier this month, researchers tested blood samples routinely collected from people younger than 18 in Mississippi between May and September 2020. Although the state had only received reports of about 9,000 COVID infections in kids through September, analyses of coronavirus antibodies in the blood  suggested that roughly 114,000 of them had actually had the pathogen—meaning the virus had infected nearly 13 times more children and adolescents than the state had recorded.
 The upshot of such findings is that the U.S. must inoculate kids if it ever wants to reach herd immunity. “If we think about the fact that [people] 18 and under actually make up 25 percent of the population of the United States and probably a bigger proportion of the world population, we really need to make sure that children are part of the population that are immune by vaccine,” says Yvonne Maldonado, a pediatric infectious disease physician at the Stanford University School of Medicine and chair of the American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) Committee on Infectious Diseases.
 And although kids are generally at low risk for COVID complications, some are not so fortunate.  More than 13,500 kids in the U.S. have been hospitalized with the disease, and 268 have died, according to AAP data released on March 18. Some of these children may have had asthma, diabetes, heart conditions, obesity, or a genetic, neurological or metabolic condition, all of which place people at  increased risk for COVID complications, the CDC says. Race and ethnicity shape risk, too: according to an August 2020 CDC study, the rate of COVID-19 hospitalization is  five times higher for Black children and eight times higher among Latino or Hispanic children than it is among white kids. Since last May, around  2,600 U.S. children and adolescents have also developed a rare condition called multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children, or MIS-C, weeks after having had COVID, and 33 of them have died.
 It is therefore crucial to have vaccines available for kids to curb the spread of the coronavirus and to protect young people who are high-risk. But “when you’re talking about putting a vaccine in children, everyone wants to make sure that it’s safe,” says Jennifer Nayak, a pediatric infectious disease physician at the University of Rochester Medical Center, who is helping to run one of Moderna’s clinical trials in kids. This means that careful clinical trials are essential, she says. Researchers also have to take special care to minimize the risks to kids who enroll in these trials. Children do not always understand the risks involved in participating in trials, so they cannot provide informed consent, Nayak says.
 This is one reason why pharmaceutical companies waited so long to begin testing their vaccines in kids. They wanted to have months’ worth of data showing that the vaccines were safe in adults first, Conway says. They also wanted to see what happened to vaccinated adults who were then exposed to the coronavirus to “make sure that if you’d been vaccinated and then got infected, that you didn’t have a bizarre, enhanced immune response to the disease,” which can be dangerous, he explains. This kind of reaction was, for instance,  observed in some children who were vaccinated with a new dengue vaccine in the Philippines in 2016 and 2017 and then exposed to the dengue virus.
 Researchers also want to be sure that the COVID vaccines do not elicit a reaction similar to MIS-C. They do not expect that result because the immune responses seen in kids with MIS-C differ from the kinds of immune responses the vaccines elicit in adults. But they still want to be sure. “Are we particularly concerned about this? No, but it’s one of the really very important reasons that the vaccines need to be studied in the pediatric population,” Nayak says. As for whether kids might have more side effects from COVID vaccines than adults do, no one yet knows. “Children may have more reactions, meaning either more fever or sore arms,” Maldonado says, “but they could be very similar.”
 To conduct the trials, researchers will first test various doses of the vaccines in small groups of kids of a particular age to determine the dose that provides protection without causing many side effects. “It's Goldilocks, essentially: this one’s too hot, this one’s too cold, this one’s just right,” Conway says. Then they will give the dose they deem best to several thousand kids and track them, along with a similar group of kids who will get a placebo shot, over time to see how likely they are to develop COVID.
 The pharmaceutical companies began trials in adolescents first because they are most similar to adults. The firms are slowly turning to younger age groups. Starting with teenagers is good from a public health standpoint, too: they are more likely than younger kids to  spread the virus and are also more likely to get  seriously ill, Nayak says. Still, the American Academy of Pediatrics maintains that even without vaccines available for young children and teens,  it can be safe to reopen schools if safety measures are taken and if community spread is limited. That recommendation was made, in part, because not having in-person school poses mental, emotional and educational risks, and when it comes to overall spread of COVID in communities, kids “don't seem to be a major driver,” Conway says.
 Researchers are not sure when vaccines for kids will be widely available because that will depend on the results of the clinical trials and the Food and Drug Administration’s approval process. But Conway says he expects them to be ready for adolescents aged 12 to 16 this summer, for five- to 11-year-olds by early 2022, and for babies and toddlers sometime after that. Maldonado hopes the vaccine for the youngest group will also be available in early 2022.
 Conway says he is pleased with how the COVID clinical trials in young people have been designed and organized in that they balance the need for both safety and speed. Children deserve to be kept safe—both in clinical trials and the real world, where they may be exposed to the disease. “We need to advocate for kids,” he says. “They deserve to be protected.”
 Read more about the coronavirus outbreak from Scientific American   here. And read coverage from our international network of magazines  here.
 *Editor’s Note (3/31/21): This sentence was updated to include results of Pfizer and BioNTech’s trial of their COVID-19 vaccine in adolescents.
title: 孩子们什么时候会得到covid疫苗？
title_en: When Will Kids Get COVID Vaccines?

吉隆坡（路透社） -  Microsoft Corporation将在马来西亚的未来五年内投入10亿美元，这是新的伙伴关系方案的一部分，以及与政府机构和当地公司的新伙伴关系，周一表示。
 关于美国科技巨头在马来西亚的最大投资的公告是在2月份之后为Microsoft，Google，Amazon和State Telecoms Contekom Malaysia提供了条件批准，以建立和管理超级数据中心并提供云服务。
 它还在该国看到外国直接投资（FDI）去年持续68％，东南亚最大的下降。
  马来西亚为一名投资目的地辩护，金融部长最近说它正在寻求帮助吸引更多外国直接投资的激励措施。
 它表示，在未来五年内，这些云服务提供商的投资将在120亿英镑和150亿林吉特（291亿美元）之间的总额。
 作为博尔山马来西亚倡议的一部分，微软将建立其第一个“DataceRe地区”，其中包括多个数据中心，在马来西亚管理来自各国的数据，总理Muhyddin Yassin告诉一项标志着该计划的启动。
  “即将到来的数据中心地区将成为马来西亚的游戏变更者，”微软执行副总裁Jean-Philippe Constois在一份声明中表示，增加它将使得政府和企业“转变”他们的运营。 
根据该计划，微软还将协助高达一百万马来西亚人在2023年底获得数字技能。
note_en:
KUALA LUMPUR (Reuters) - Microsoft Corporation will invest $1 billion over the next five years in Malaysia as part of a new partnership programme with government agencies and local companies, the Southeast Asian nation’s prime minister said on Monday.
 The announcement on what would be the U.S. tech giant’s biggest investment in Malaysia comes after the country in February gave conditional approvals for Microsoft, Google, Amazon and state telecoms firm Telekom Malaysia to build and manage hyper-scale data centres and provide cloud services.
 It also comes after the country saw foreign direct investments (FDI) plunge by 68% last year, the biggest decline in Southeast Asia.
  Malaysia has defended itself as an investment destination, with the finance minister recently saying it was looking at incentives to help attract more FDI.
 It has said the investments from these cloud service providers will total between 12 billion ringgit and 15 billion ringgit ($2.91 billion-$3.64 billion) over the next five years.
 As part of the Bersama Malaysia initiative, Microsoft will establish its first “datacentre region”, which consists of multiple data centres, in Malaysia to manage data from various countries, Prime Minister Muhyiddin Yassin told an event marking the launch of the programme.
  “The upcoming datacenter region will be a game-changer for Malaysia,” Microsoft Executive Vice President Jean-Philippe Courtois said in a statement, adding it will enable the government and businesses to “transform” their operations.
 Under the programme, Microsoft will also assist up to a million Malaysians in getting digital skills by the end of 2023.
title: 微软将在马来西亚建立其第一个数据中心地区，是其博尔山马来西亚倡议的一部分，旨在在2023年底之前技能1M马来西亚
title_en: Microsoft to establish its first data center region in Malaysia as part of its Bersama Malaysia initiative, which aims to skill 1M Malaysians by the end of 2023

我是一个内战一般和铁路巨头，抬头看着科罗拉多州中部的帕克斯峰，并决定“他想建造一个符合壮丽景色的城市”。虽然说，科罗拉多斯普林斯市长约翰·萨克斯说，镇帕尔默最终会发现在落矶山脉的脚下。现在，150年后，Suthers先生表示，科罗拉多斯普林斯的居民仍在努力建造山上的闪亮城市。但最近，该建筑已经加快了。
  中小型城市的房价在美国山西迅速上升。科罗拉多斯普林斯的平均房价在2020年2月和2021年2月之间增加了15％;博塞曼，蒙大拿州的价格增加了近20％（见图）。在Boise中，爱达荷州的价格上涨28％，这是一个在线列表平台Zillow追踪的900地铁地区的最大增加。
 将此令人诱人地归结为大流行，在其他地方的房价上涨的一个因素。锁定显示办公室工作人员，他们不需要通往一个中央商务区的花哨总部来完成工作。这让一些想知道为什么他们在旧金山一卧室公寓出去。靠近美国广阔的空间和山西西方提供的社会遥远的户外活动。梅根·劳森，博兹曼的智商经济学，博兹曼智库表示，Covid-19危机表明，西方的神话是活着的。当人们在想呼吸室时，她说，“他们描绘了这些非常标志性的景观。”
  大流行病可能确实有兴趣增强，但人口繁荣一段时间显而易见。人口普查局估计，2015年至2020年全国十大增长州的七个在西方。 Tatiana Bailey（科罗拉多大学科罗拉多州）斯普林斯经济学家Tatiana Bailey表示，十年下半年的经济增长迅速也反映了该地区的巨大恢复。科罗拉多斯普林斯等城市正击中他们的步伐，就像流行病一样。
 其他三个因素是贡献房价的上涨。首先，西方落后于建造高密度住房。那是改变的。在Pikes Peak Region中，几乎许多多家别单位将从年度到3月31日作为单家族家庭发布的许可。
 二，购房者和投资者正在博兹曼等地方购买物业，并用现金支付，有时候看不见，说明劳森女士说。因为如果没有涉及贷款人，则不需要评估，所以现金购买可以充气当地住房市场。最后，房价与当地劳动力市场之间存在越来越脱颖而出。在旧金山获得一家公司的支票时定居博伊西的偏远工人可以提供超过博伊西预算的人。
  经济适用房提供的减少是令人担忧的当地人和政策制定者。建立更多多家庭单位将帮助否则将售出市场的长期居民，但只有新的公寓只提供一系列收入。豪华公寓不会做诀窍。同时，无家可归，正在上升。爱达荷亚住房和金融协会估计，博伊西地区的无家可归者人数在2016年和2020年之间增加了203％。 
山区城市的增长正在改变周围的各国。科罗拉多斯普林斯，丹佛，博尔德和柯林斯堡沿着科罗拉多州的正面范围形成一座城市走廊，恰好位于落基山脉。随着他们的人群增加，城市已变得更加联系。 “我不认为我们是18年前在这里移动的丹佛卧室社区，”科罗拉多斯普林斯的房地产经纪人Jeremy Wynia说。 “我们绝对是现在。”
 融合地铁地区的融合将继续。科罗拉多州的州去世师伊丽莎白加纳表示，87％的2.1米的人认为百年州的百年人看起来将达到2050年将居住在前方范围内。拟议的铁路将进一步链接四个城市。爱达荷的宝石山谷，只是俄勒冈州边境的东部，目睹了类似类似的东西，因为快速增长针织一起博伊西，经络和Nampa。
 作为这些城市的人口统计，他们的政治也是如此。 Suckers，共和党和劳伦·麦克莱恩议员都说，博伊塞式的民主市长表示，他们的城市已经变得更加自由，因为他们已经成长了。在科罗拉多斯普林斯承认这一点是历来一直是保守主义的堡垒。
 所有这些改变都用悖论展示了山西。该地区的城市必须建立和扩大以适应人口增长，但在此过程中，他们可能会危及人们在那里移动的东西：空间，生活质量和旷野的障碍。 McLean女士表示，人们会陷入博伊西“以远离大城市的问题”。但是博伊西（人口：230,000和崛起）可能成为一个大城市。 ■

I , a civil-war general and railway magnate, looked up at Pikes Peak in central Colorado and decided “he wanted to build a city that matched the magnificent scenery”. Or so says John Suthers, the mayor of Colorado Springs, the town Palmer would eventually found at the foot of the Rocky Mountains. Now, 150 years later, Mr Suthers says the residents of Colorado Springs are still trying to build that shining city on a hill. Lately, though, the building has sped up.
  House prices in small and midsized cities are rising rapidly in America’s Mountain West. Average home values in Colorado Springs rose by 15% between February 2020 and February 2021; prices in Bozeman, Montana, increased by nearly 20% (see chart). In Boise, Idaho house prices are up by 28%, the biggest increase among the 900 metro areas tracked by Zillow, an online listings platform.
 It is tempting to attribute this to the pandemic, a factor in  house-price rises elsewhere. Lockdowns showed office workers that they didn’t need to commute to a fancy headquarters in a central business district to do their jobs. That left some wondering why they were forking out for a one-bedroom flat in San Francisco. The proximity to America’s wide-open spaces and the socially distant outdoor activities that the Mountain West offers is alluring. Megan Lawson, of Headwaters Economics, a think-tank in Bozeman, says the covid-19 crisis has shown that the mythology of the West is alive and well. When people are thinking of breathing room, she says, “they picture these very iconic landscapes.”
  The pandemic may indeed have boosted interest, but a population boom has been apparent for some time. The   Census Bureau estimates that seven of the ten fastest-growing states in the country from 2015 to 2020 were in the West. Rapid economic growth in the second half of the decade also reflects the region’s slower recovery from the Great Recession, says Tatiana Bailey, an economist at the University of Colorado Colorado Springs. Cities such as Colorado Springs were hitting their stride just as the pandemic struck.
 Three other factors are contributing to the uptick in house prices. First, the West has lagged in building high-density housing. That is changing. In the Pikes Peak region, nearly as many multi-family units will be built from the permits issued in the year to March 31st as single-family homes.
 Second, homebuyers and investors are buying property in places like Bozeman and paying with cash, sometimes sight unseen, says Ms Lawson. Because appraisals aren’t required if a lender isn’t involved, cash purchases can inflate local housing markets. Lastly, there is a growing disconnect between house prices and local labour markets. Remote workers who settle in Boise while getting a pay cheque from a firm in San Francisco can afford much more than someone on a Boise budget.
  The diminishing supply of affordable housing is worrying locals and policymakers. Building more multi-family units will help longtime residents who would otherwise be priced out of the market, but only if the new apartments serve a range of incomes. Luxury condos won’t do the trick. Homelessness, meanwhile, is on the rise. The Idaho Housing and Finance Association estimates that the number of homeless people in the Boise area rose by 203% between 2016 and 2020.
 The growth of the Mountain West’s cities is transforming the states around them. Colorado Springs, Denver, Boulder and Fort Collins form an urban corridor along Colorado’s Front Range, just east of the Rockies. As their populations have increased, the cities have become more connected. “I don’t think we were a bedroom community of Denver when I moved here 18 years ago,” says Jeremy Wynia, a real-estate agent in Colorado Springs. “We absolutely are now.”
 That melding of metro areas will probably continue. Elizabeth Garner, Colorado’s state demographer, says 87% of the 2.1m people the Centennial State looks set to gain by 2050 will live on the Front Range. A proposed railway would further link the four cities. Idaho’s Treasure Valley, just east of the Oregon border, is witnessing something similar as rapid growth knits together Boise, Meridian and Nampa.
 As the demography of these cities shifts, so do their politics. Both Mr Suthers, a Republican, and Lauren McLean, Boise’s Democratic mayor, say their cities have become more liberal as they have grown. That is no small thing to admit in Colorado Springs, which has historically been a bastion of conservatism.
 All this change presents the Mountain West with a paradox. The region’s cities have to build and expand to accommodate population growth, but in doing so they may jeopardise the very things people move there for: space, quality of life and proximity to wilderness. Ms McLean says people come to Boise “to get away from the problems of larger cities”. But Boise (population: 230,000 and rising) may become a big city yet.  ■
    This article appeared in the United States section of the print edition under the headline "Growing pains"
title: 美国山西的小城市正在蓬勃发展
title_en: Small cities in America’s Mountain West are booming

二十一世纪的第三十年将是一个狂野的骑行。在地球上的良好标准程序将会有差，但大流行和其并发恶魔无意中引发了人类聪明才智的反障，这些人的聪明才智通过弥补的工作，技术和更多的楼层破裂。不仅仅是因为加速技术领养，而且（也许更重要的是）可能是可能的，因为overton窗口是如何安排我们的生活的扩大，至少在短暂的时刻。
 您甚至可能会称之为咆哮的二十岁，而是在禁止突袭期间将您的植物放在植物中，而不是在工厂中倾倒饮酒。改变的事情越多，他们越来越同样，嗯？
 当然有一件事并没有改变是威力的工业综合体，中学，次级机构和凯特林·弗拉纳人和威廉德雷斯·威廉·德雷斯韦兹的品牌公司已经写得这么好。
 这些是您的哈佛大学，您的Yales，您的许多咨询公司和法学院以及某些技术公司等。
 他们承诺识别和实现最佳和最聪明的最聪明，而且不可避免地转出箱子检查，规则的遵循，可选择的保留，不安全，风险厌恶，抑郁，无聊的成年人恰好恰好控制的社会中的金额和权力。
 但是，前一段之外，这篇文章不是关于燃烧系统，因为这是一个傻瓜的差事。这是关于释放其中的一些人，因为这一奇妙的十年不是时候在副中拥有一个人的灵魂。我不寻求革命，而是宁愿复兴。
   我在2019年搬到纽约市之后，我和一个漂亮的男人在约会。他去了一个鸽友的常规之一，现在为这座城市的一个鸽友咨询公司工作了。在讨论他的工作后有点咖啡，它变得丰富地清楚，他并没有真正喜欢他的工作。但他说，这很好，因为它付出了很好，如果他想，可以让他在未来工作的客户端。 
但为什么他没有试图弄清楚他真正做的那样，或者喜欢得多，并这样做，我问道。
  我问道，在他在他目前的工作年份工作年份之前或之后，我问过多年的客户端。
   读者，他仍然在几年后工作，没有离开的迹象。我会感到震惊，因为他缺乏迫切地寻找一个值得生活的生活，特别是在他的优势，如果我没有看到同样的事情已经发挥了一百次。地狱，我知道有很多文凭和曲线破坏的人，那些一直计划在十年内离开工作。
 他们不离开，因为，在一天结束时，他们不能让自己冒着所获得的声望冒险，这是另一种说法：他们不能冒失去外部验证的风险，威望工业综合体的驱动力。毕竟，声望只不过是一群人的集体尊重（基于人们倾向于找到着名的人，他们并不是很擅长与德国或荣誉，甚至智慧相匹配声望）。
 您拥有这些人，柏拉图式或浪漫，专业或随意的更封闭的谈话，您认为自己的阳痿就越多。不仅仅是金色的手铐，他们是迈出迈达斯的全部恐怖，同时坚持认为它们可以单独吃金子来生存。
 但他们应该聪明吗？一个奇迹。如果他们真的是最好的，因为他们的文凭和收入索赔，为什么他们不能逃脱？为什么他们不能雕刻自己的道路？他们为什么不尝试？在他们所有的能力和累积状态，为什么他们不努力结束或改革保持雄伟，雄心勃勃，年轻人进入梦幻曲面的文化制度和机构？
 答案因个人和背景而异，但其中一些原因：道德怯懦，缺乏想象力，威望工业综合体的心理调理，以及一些版本的“你不能让一个人理解他的薪水要求他不明白的东西。 “ 
首先，我正在销售文凭，因为这是一种努力达到的方式。意图宣言和隶属关系，而不是发布它的机构或其品牌，而是对它应该代表的理想。
 其次，它是一个放弃的种类，较大的威望工业综合体以及对人的作用，它如何在扭曲较大的世界时变形。
 一旦你遇到足够的哈佛大学校友（虽然你可以替代任何数量的机构和公司），你意识到文凭不是一个可靠的质量指标，不仅是因为它的发行人承认没有学者严谨的个人，但是，由于该机构本身通过提交对威胁生态系统的狭隘要求来激励毕业生以降低自己的知识分子和个人质量。
 一旦你意识到这一点，文凭就开始类似于垄断金钱。您必须在逐个案例的基础上开始评估人，而不是使用程度作为可靠的代理。任何固体货币都可以承受一定数量的伪造或贬值的循环硬币，但只有一个点。而哈佛大学的威尔望远岛工业综合设施一直削减其廉价金属的股票。
  最后，这是一个对别人的灯塔，或者那些可能是那些人。生活比我想象的更漂亮，它可能在成长。梦想更生动，哲学更有启发，代理商更加激动。天堂？不，给我地球。
 但是，你的军团被困在生活中，不再激励你，而你被默认接受的人为心理激励结构的人为。光已经走出了你的眼睛，比放弃改变宏观世界 - 你已经放弃了你的个人世界，你自己的幸福。如果你对你认为“幸福”是一个天真的目标，那么你可能已经太过分了。在你们其余的：你可能从来没有知道过辐射人类的快乐之花，虽然你对夏普的尖锐性密切熟悉，但在他人所定义的游戏中非常竞争的胜利。
 如果这张照片是外国的，那就去你享受的生活，更好;如果你不这样做，我并不试着说服你讨厌你的生活。但对于你们中的许多人来说：在你的生活中，在你的生活中，着名和高薪，你将有一个黑暗的灵魂之夜。你会知道它的时候：你会怨恨你的工作，你＆＃39; ll问题整个系统，想知道为什么人类应该像这样生活，你会想知道为什么你的同事 - 谁不喜欢他们的工作 - 为他人的优秀职业选择。你会想到你鄙视谁的重复性，他们或你的。 
您对您的体验进行了适当的反应，对圣灵的免疫反应，因为系统确实如此糟糕。
 你不是疯了（而是它是世界上的世界已经发疯了），但你将独自一人。你周围的每个人都会不成比例地告诉你处理它，它真的很糟糕，那些福利值得成本，如果你愿意，你可以在几年内离开（但环顾四周，看看谁真的做，并以哪种方式）。
  许多人，当他们进入世界上最负盛名的学校和赞誉的公司时，设想新的和不同的现实，或者至少比他们来自的那个更好的生活。他们允许社会系统及其激励措施携带它们，结果是如此可预测，以便几乎不可避免。曾经有能力最生动的梦想的人发现他们的想象力和好奇心被摧毁，他们对欢乐的欢乐所取代的尊重和零奖励的期望。
 这篇论文是一个解释，这是一种吸引力。给谁？那些认识到自己情况的人反映在他们身上。生活可以否则。您可以快乐和内容，您可以与幸福的其他人围绕自己，而世界可以为我们所有人做得更好。如何做到这一点，每个人都是特定的，但它是可靠和反复的。细节是离散的，方法一般。
 对于许多人在我的预期受众中，第一个关键是让自己摆脱您的凭据，并与他所做的其他人会面。拥有着名的程度或纯粹的简历可以在你的头脑中放置一个不断问的声音，“我有一个[名字 - 品牌的谱系]，别人会想到什么，如果我[...]，它永远不会给出一个慈善者预报。此外，它惩罚好奇心，独立和初学者的思想，“如果你尝试新的东西并失败，那么你的血统有什么看法？你不是那么好，是吗？“这是一个Procrustean的路径依赖，只允许您通过暴徒批准的行动，这是声望的大生成者。
  正如我上面提到的那样，我并不兴趣撕毁声望工业综合体。为什么？
 它太嵌入了社会（和人性）才能完成。删除它充分意味着改变了使其有价值的心理和经济激励措施。复杂的学校，大学，公司的可见机关 - 只是图片的一部分。 
我不想去花哨的学校或为一个花哨的公司工作本质上是对还是错的;我们的世界充满了遭受的损害和不利的机构。这并不意味着你停止与世界互动，但你不必喂野兽。如果你发现自己越来越倾向于你的血统来自我价值，而且挥舞它而不是你自己的角色，以获得你想要的东西，如果你自我审查，以避免冒着遗忘或站立的损失，如果你拒绝机会，因为他们不看起来像是一个“加强”的声望，你会纠正怀疑你现在是问题的一部分。你是坏人。
 这是一个机会成本的问题。为什么将所有努力从外面延迟到系统（不太可能的东西），当我可以努力让自己成为一个更好的人并从内部支持更好的系统（可能会开始恢复旧复合体的竞争） ？自2020年以来，有各种新的系统和人群，在线和休息，这已经绽放（我最喜欢的是AnnaGát的intertentellect），他们基于威奇工业综合体仅在理论上拥有的价值观：好奇心，冒险，相互支持和真理寻求。
 我想我在这里刚招募了威廉工业综合体的其他叛逃者。系统无法自行变得更好，但我们肯定可以以各种方式实现。如果它可以让它抓住我们。
 现在是建造的时候; “改变世界”是最好的，成为自己的好人。为什么文艺复兴在这里选择革命？
 我会以一些常见问题解答结束，我已经有关这件整个狗和小马表演我正在投入，但请不要忘记在政府中讨论哈佛大学课程，2014年班级的价格。稍微用。
  但是，丹尼尔，你不知道卖你的文凭是否实际上没有转移潜在程度？是的，我知道这一点。在许多情况下，“潜在程度”比文凭本身在许多情况下都不太真实。那是......这整件事。
 如果我购买文凭，我会得到什么？这是政府的荣誉学位，在四年之后获得了四年（我甚至在分裂上写了一篇论文）。如果你买它，我会把我的名字交掉，在我整洁的新斯宾塞剧本中写下你的剧本，然后将自己的签名贴在大师院长下方。可以确定其他奖励物。 
这不是一个宣传特技吗？好吧，不只是，但是的，它肯定是。我想提请注意我所呈现的想法（以及那些我继续出席的想法），我希望累积更多的社会/硬资本来维持它们。
 但它是艺术吗？ 👽✌️🚬这是性能艺术，也许，取决于您的定义以及所有这些定义。艺术是概念性的混凝土，通过意图和媒介的组合直接向另一个方式传输想法。但这种表现需要两个完成，我会爱一个伴侣。
 这是一项投资：在我这里概述的想法，在我身上。我是这样的，我和我一起工作;如果你想看到更多，你会得到更多。有些人在公司销售股票，我有一张纸张，我认为可能是兴趣。
 这是一个伸出的手：当他们到达十字路口时，另一个哈佛（或任何地方）毕业生的生活会有时间。他们将实现他们对他们开放的学位的世界毫无生气。一切都会抱怨，没有人会开心。当那个时候来了，我希望他们记得读这个，实现他们并不孤单，看看有更好的生活方式。赚取凭证可能是艰苦的工作，但凭证不应该决定你的生活过程。
 这是一项警告：顾问，金融家，律师（也是其他人，茎和医学也不是安全的，他们只是我的本地领土） - 我已经与你们中的许多人一起去过了，并与闭门落后于更多。我充分意识到你们很多人讨厌你的工作，你不喜欢你的生活。更糟糕的是，我可以看到你的思想是多么愚蠢地受到声望。你真的无法想象出不同的生活方式;你宁愿遭受整个温水，而不是找到另一种方式。你很无聊。就像酗酒过得太过甚大，你对这种情况的醉酒意识无法拯救你，只能作为无助的见证人。你的队长是链条：人工道路依赖者说你不能在你开始后做任何不负责任的事情。但世界大于那个;如果他们想要，有些邦克人甚至可以拍卖他们的哈佛文凭。
 这是一个庆祝：无处不在互联网上，卓越存在，无论是何处或谁，它都会变得更加易于易于途径。精英机构将继续成为其中一些的来源 - 不是因为机构是优越的，而是因为优秀的个人自我选择，然后生成scenius。但是一个更光明的未来对于一个举起各种人才和美德的社会，而不仅仅是菲亚特卓越的文凭常见。在自己：在你生命中的所有领域培育美丽和美德，而不仅仅是赚取奖金的零件。在别人：学会概括地识别卓越，而不依赖凭据。
 这是Lulz：生活在其灿烂的品种中是美妙的，在纯粹的体积中是可能的。宝贝，我们要去火星！有规则，但你可以写新的并忽略他人。为什么我不应该试图卖掉哈佛文凭，以结交新朋友，加油我的礼物，改变我的未来？与NFT不同，这是你实际上可以带回家的东西。 
如果你有兴趣，我们应该相互了解。我的推特DMS是开放的。或者如果您有一定的幽默感，您可以使用LinkedIn。
 当我第一次想到这样做时，我嘲笑自己。多么荒谬！但喜剧是悲剧加上的时间，悲剧是哈佛学位（更广泛地，流氓的证据）对曾经拥有梦想能力的人的思想。二十一世纪的第三个十年将是令人难以置信的，并且没有更好的时机导致反抗对抗旧神灵。
         对于夏季喂养常春藤联盟的系统的急剧批评，阅读凯特琳·弗拉纳曼在大西洋中的作品，“私立学校已成为淫秽”（从2021年3月）。
 对于最近哈佛毕业生的校园招聘文化，见藏红花黄的钯，“哈佛大学创造了经理代替精英”（从2020年7月开始）。
 这件作品（2008）是推出一千人的论文：“美国学者的”精英教育的缺点“是威廉德莱塞斯的金色标准，用于描述金字塔顶部的特定事项。他把它扩展到一本书（见下文）。
